title: Strongly connected components Tarjan
description: Find Strongly Connected Components using Brute Force, Kosaraju and Tarjan algorithms

examples.explanation: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[2,3], 2:[0], 3:[]}. Step 2: Compute reachability matrix. From 0: DFS visits 0,1,2,3 → reach[0]=[T,T,T,T]. From 1: DFS visits 1,2,0,3 → reach[1]=[T,T,T,T]. From 2: DFS visits 2,0,1,3 → reach[2]=[T,T,T,T]. From 3: DFS visits 3 → reach[3]=[F,F,F,T]. Step 3: Find SCCs. Start u=0: Check v=0: reach[0][0]=T, reach[0][0]=T → add 0. v=1: reach[0][1]=T, reach[1][0]=T → add 1. v=2: reach[0][2]=T, reach[2][0]=T → add 2. v=3: reach[0][3]=T, reach[3][0]=F → skip. SCC=[0,1,2], mark 0,1,2 processed. Next u=3 (not processed): Check v=3: reach[3][3]=T, reach[3][3]=T → add 3. SCC=[3]. Output: [[0,1,2],[3]].

Step 1: Adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[]}. Step 2: Reachability. From 0: DFS visits 0,1,2 → reach[0]=[T,T,T]. From 1: DFS visits 1,2 → reach[1]=[F,T,T]. From 2: DFS visits 2 → reach[2]=[F,F,T]. Step 3: Find SCCs. u=0: v=0: reach[0][0]=T, reach[0][0]=T → add 0. v=1: reach[0][1]=T, reach[1][0]=F → skip. v=2: reach[0][2]=T, reach[2][0]=F → skip. SCC=[0], mark 0 processed. u=1: v=1: reach[1][1]=T, reach[1][1]=T → add 1. v=2: reach[1][2]=T, reach[2][1]=F → skip. SCC=[1], mark 1 processed. u=2: v=2: reach[2][2]=T, reach[2][2]=T → add 2. SCC=[2]. Output: [[0],[1],[2]].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(V*(V+E))
approaches.summary: For each vertex, perform DFS to find all reachable vertices, then check mutual reachability to identify SCCs. This is straightforward but inefficient for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force SCC Identification
ladders.desc: Find all strongly connected components in a directed graph using brute force mutual reachability checks.
ladders.explanation: This brute force approach identifies SCCs by checking every pair of vertices for mutual reachability. The intuition is that in an SCC, every vertex can reach every other vertex. We first compute reachability from each vertex using DFS, storing results in a 2D matrix. Then, we group vertices where reachability[u][v] and reachability[v][u] are both true. The algorithm works because SCCs are equivalence classes under mutual reachability. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize V×V reachability matrix as False. 2) For each vertex u, perform DFS to mark all vertices reachable from u as True in reachability[u][*]. 3) For each vertex u, initialize an empty SCC list. 4) For each vertex v, if reachability[u][v] and reachability[v][u] are True, add v to u's SCC. 5) Collect unique SCCs. Time: O(V×(V+E)) for V DFS traversals each O(V+E). Space: O(V²) for matrix plus O(V+E) for graph. Connection to L1: This uses the reachability computation from L1 as its core subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling visited arrays properly in DFS (must reset per source), duplicate SCCs in output (use visited flags to skip processed vertices), and misunderstanding mutual reachability (both directions must be true). Real code pattern: def brute_force_scc(graph): V = len(graph); reach = [[False]*V for _ in range(V)]; for u in range(V): visited = [False]*V; dfs(u, graph, visited); for v in range(V): reach[u][v] = visited[v]; sccs = []; processed = [False]*V; for u in range(V): if not processed[u]: scc = []; for v in range(V): if reach[u][v] and reach[v][u] and not processed[v]: scc.append(v); processed[v] = True; if scc: sccs.append(scc); return sccs

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[2,3], 2:[0], 3:[]}. Step 2: Compute reachability matrix. From 0: DFS visits 0,1,2,3 → reach[0]=[T,T,T,T]. From 1: DFS visits 1,2,0,3 → reach[1]=[T,T,T,T]. From 2: DFS visits 2,0,1,3 → reach[2]=[T,T,T,T]. From 3: DFS visits 3 → reach[3]=[F,F,F,T]. Step 3: Find SCCs. Start u=0: Check v=0: reach[0][0]=True, reach[0][0]=True → add 0. v=1: reach[0][1]=True, reach[1][0]=True → add 1. v=2: reach[0][2]=True, reach[2][0]=True → add 2. v=3: reach[0][3]=True, reach[3][0]=False → skip. SCC=[0,1,2], mark 0,1,2 processed. Next u=3 (not processed): Check v=3: reach[3][3]=True, reach[3][3]=True → add 3. SCC=[3]. Output: [[0,1,2],[3]].

Step 1: Adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[]}. Step 2: Reachability. From 0: DFS visits 0,1,2 → reach[0]=[T,T,T]. From 1: DFS visits 1,2 → reach[1]=[F,T,T]. From 2: DFS visits 2 → reach[2]=[F,F,T]. Step 3: Find SCCs. u=0: v=0: reach[0][0]=True, reach[0][0]=True → add 0. v=1: reach[0][1]=True, reach[1][0]=False → skip. v=2: reach[0][2]=True, reach[2][0]=False → skip. SCC=[0], mark 0 processed. u=1: v=1: reach[1][1]=True, reach[1][1]=True → add 1. v=2: reach[1][2]=True, reach[2][1]=False → skip. SCC=[1], mark 1 processed. u=2: v=2: reach[2][2]=True, reach[2][2]=True → add 2. SCC=[2]. Output: [[0],[1],[2]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Reachability from a Source Vertex
ladders.desc: Perform DFS from a given source vertex to find all vertices reachable from it.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes which vertices are reachable from a single source vertex using DFS. The intuition is that by traversing the graph depth-first from the source, we can explore all possible paths, marking each visited vertex as reachable. This is fundamental because SCC identification requires knowing reachability between all pairs of vertices. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a visited array of size V, all False. 2) Mark the source vertex as visited. 3) Use a stack (or recursion) to explore: push source, then while stack not empty, pop vertex u, for each neighbor v of u, if not visited, mark visited and push v. 4) The visited array now indicates reachability from source. Time: O(V+E) for one DFS traversal. Space: O(V) for visited array plus O(V) stack in worst case. Connection to L0: This is called V times in L0 to build the complete reachability matrix. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark source as visited initially, not using directed edges correctly (follow adjacency list as given), and stack overflow in recursion for large graphs (use iterative DFS). Real code pattern: def dfs_reachable(source, graph): V = len(graph); visited = [False]*V; stack = [source]; visited[source] = True; while stack: u = stack.pop(); for v in graph[u]: if not visited[v]: visited[v] = True; stack.append(v); return visited

examples.trace: Step 1: Adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[0]}. Step 2: Initialize visited=[False,False,False], stack=[0], visited[0]=True. Step 3: Pop 0, neighbors: [1]. 1 not visited → visited[1]=True, stack=[1]. Step 4: Pop 1, neighbors: [2]. 2 not visited → visited[2]=True, stack=[2]. Step 5: Pop 2, neighbors: [0]. 0 visited → skip. Stack empty. Final visited=[True,True,True].

Step 1: Adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[]}. Step 2: visited=[False,False,False], stack=[1], visited[1]=True. Step 3: Pop 1, neighbors: [2]. 2 not visited → visited[2]=True, stack=[2]. Step 4: Pop 2, neighbors: [] → skip. Stack empty. Final visited=[False,True,True].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Mutual Reachability Check for SCC Membership
ladders.desc: Determine if two vertices are in the same SCC by checking bidirectional reachability.
ladders.explanation: This core logic determines SCC membership by checking if two vertices can reach each other. The intuition is that in a directed graph, vertices u and v are in the same SCC if and only if there is a path from u to v and from v to u. This property defines SCCs as equivalence classes. We use precomputed reachability data (e.g., from a matrix) to test this condition efficiently. Step-by-step: Given reachability matrix reach where reach[u][v] is True if v is reachable from u. For vertices u and v, they are in the same SCC if reach[u][v] and reach[v][u] are both True. This works because mutual reachability is transitive: if u reaches v and v reaches w, and v reaches u and w reaches v, then u and w are mutually reachable. Time: O(1) per pair check if matrix is precomputed. Space: O(1) for the check. Connection to L1: This uses the reachability results from L1's DFS. Common mistakes: Confusing reachability with direct edges (need paths, not single edges), not ensuring both directions are checked, and misinterpreting matrix indices. Real code pattern: def in_same_scc(u, v, reach): return reach[u][v] and reach[v][u]

examples.input: reach matrix: row0=[True,True,True], row1=[True,True,True], row2=[True,True,True]; u=0, v=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Check reach[0][1] = True (from row0[1]). Step 2: Check reach[1][0] = True (from row1[0]). Step 3: Both True → return True.

examples.input: reach matrix: row0=[True,True,True], row1=[False,True,True], row2=[False,False,True]; u=0, v=2
examples.trace: Step 1: Check reach[0][2] = True (from row0[2]). Step 2: Check reach[2][0] = False (from row2[0]). Step 3: Not both True → return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and DFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Represent a directed graph and perform a basic DFS to visit all reachable vertices.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves representing a directed graph and using DFS to traverse it. The intuition is that graphs model relationships (edges) between entities (vertices), and DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, which is ideal for finding reachable vertices. We use an adjacency list: a list of lists where index u contains neighbors of u. DFS uses a stack (LIFO) to manage traversal: start at a vertex, mark visited, push neighbors, repeat. Step-by-step: 1) Build adjacency list from edges. 2) Initialize visited array. 3) Push start vertex to stack, mark visited. 4) While stack not empty: pop vertex u, for each neighbor v in adjacency[u], if not visited, mark visited and push v. Time: O(V+E) for full traversal. Space: O(V+E) for adjacency list plus O(V) for visited and stack. Connection to L2: This provides the reachability data needed for mutual checks. Common mistakes: Using adjacency matrix (inefficient for sparse graphs), forgetting to mark visited before pushing to stack (causing duplicates), and not handling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: def dfs_traversal(start, adj): visited = [False]*len(adj); stack = [start]; visited[start] = True; while stack: u = stack.pop(); for v in adj[u]: if not visited[v]: visited[v] = True; stack.append(v); return visited

examples.trace: Step 1: Adjacency: [[1],[2],[0]]. Step 2: visited=[False,False,False], stack=[0], visited[0]=True. Step 3: Pop 0 → neighbors [1]: 1 not visited → visited[1]=True, stack=[1]. Step 4: Pop 1 → neighbors [2]: 2 not visited → visited[2]=True, stack=[2]. Step 5: Pop 2 → neighbors [0]: 0 visited → skip. Stack empty. Visited=[True,True,True].

Step 1: Adjacency: [[1],[2],[]]. Step 2: visited=[False,False,False], stack=[0], visited[0]=True. Step 3: Pop 0 → neighbors [1]: 1 not visited → visited[1]=True, stack=[1]. Step 4: Pop 1 → neighbors [2]: 2 not visited → visited[2]=True, stack=[2]. Step 5: Pop 2 → neighbors [] → skip. Stack empty. Visited=[True,True,True].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over Graph Vertices and Edges
ladders.desc: Perform basic operations on graph vertices and edges, such as accessing neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves iterating through vertices and their adjacent edges in a graph. The intuition is that graphs are collections of vertices connected by edges, and processing them requires looping over these elements. For a directed graph with V vertices and E edges, we use data structures like adjacency lists. Key operations: iterate over all vertices (0 to V-1), and for each vertex u, iterate over its neighbors (edges from u). This is fundamental for traversal algorithms. Step-by-step: Given adjacency list adj where adj[u] is a list of neighbors: for u in range(V): for v in adj[u]: process edge u→v. Time: O(V+E) to visit all vertices and edges. Space: O(1) for iteration. Connection to L3: This is used in DFS to explore neighbors. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in vertex indices (0-based vs 1-based), modifying adjacency list during iteration, and assuming undirected edges. Real code pattern: for u in range(len(adj)): for v in adj[u]: # do something with edge u→v

examples.trace: Step 1: Adjacency: [[1],[2],[]]. V=3. Step 2: u=0: neighbors [1] → edge 0→1. Step 3: u=1: neighbors [2] → edge 1→2. Step 4: u=2: neighbors [] → no edge. Total edges: 2.
Step 1: Adjacency: [[1],[0]]. V=2. Step 2: u=0: neighbors [1] → edge 0→1. Step 3: u=1: neighbors [0] → edge 1→0. Total edges: 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Directed Graphs and Vertices
ladders.desc: Learn what directed graphs and vertices are, and how to represent them simply.
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces directed graphs and vertices. A graph is a set of vertices (nodes) connected by edges. In a directed graph, edges have a direction: an edge from vertex u to vertex v means you can go from u to v, but not necessarily vice versa. Vertices are typically labeled with numbers (0,1,2,...). We represent a graph using an adjacency list: a list where each element corresponds to a vertex and contains a list of its neighbors (vertices it points to). For example, graph with vertices 0,1,2 and edges 0→1, 1→2 is represented as [[1], [2], []]. This means vertex 0 has neighbor 1, vertex 1 has neighbor 2, vertex 2 has no neighbors. The intuition is that this structure efficiently shows connections. Step-by-step: 1) Identify all vertices (e.g., 0 to V-1). 2) For each edge u→v, add v to the list for u. Time: O(E) to build. Space: O(V+E). Connection to L4: This representation enables iteration over vertices and edges. Common mistakes: Confusing directed with undirected edges, forgetting empty lists for vertices with no outgoing edges, and using 1-based indexing inconsistently. Real code pattern: V = number of vertices; adj = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for edge in edges: u, v = edge; adj[u].append(v)

examples.trace: Step 1: V=3, initialize adj = [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Edge 0→1: add 1 to adj[0] → adj=[[1],[],[]]. Step 3: Edge 1→2: add 2 to adj[1] → adj=[[1],[2],[]]. Final: [[1],[2],[]].
Step 1: V=2, adj = [[],[]]. Step 2: Edge 0→1: add 1 to adj[0] → adj=[[1],[]]. Step 3: Edge 1→0: add 0 to adj[1] → adj=[[1],[0]]. Final: [[1],[0]].

approaches.name: Kosaraju's Algorithm O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform two DFS passes: first on the original graph to get finishing times, then on the transpose graph to output SCCs. It leverages graph reversal and ordering for efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find SCCs using Kosaraju's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Kosaraju's algorithm to find all strongly connected components in a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: Kosaraju's algorithm finds strongly connected components (SCCs) in a directed graph using two DFS passes. The key insight is that if we perform DFS on the original graph to get finishing times, then reverse the graph and perform DFS in decreasing order of finishing times, each DFS tree in the second pass will correspond to one SCC. This works because reversing the graph preserves SCCs but changes reachability between different SCCs. The algorithm has three main steps: 1) Perform DFS on the original graph, pushing vertices to a stack as they finish. 2) Reverse all edges in the graph. 3) Perform DFS on the reversed graph, popping vertices from the stack, with each DFS tree forming one SCC. Time complexity is O(V+E) since we perform two DFS traversals. Space complexity is O(V+E) for adjacency lists and stack. Common mistakes include not marking vertices as visited in both passes, incorrect graph reversal, or misunderstanding the stack ordering. The algorithm is elegant because it leverages simple graph operations to solve a complex connectivity problem.

examples.trace: Step 1: First DFS on original graph. Start at 0: 0→1→2 (back to 0, cycle). Push 2,1,0 to stack. Continue from 3: 3→4. Push 4,3. Stack: [2,4,3,1,0] (bottom to top). Step 2: Reverse graph: 1→0, 2→1, 0→2, 3→1, 4→3. Step 3: Second DFS on reversed graph. Pop 4: DFS from 4 visits only 4 (SCC {4}). Pop 3: DFS from 3 visits 3 only (SCC {3}). Pop 2: DFS from 2 visits 2→1→0 (SCC {0,1,2}). Result: [[0,1,2], [3], [4]].

Step 1: First DFS on original graph. Start at 0: 0→1→2→3→0 (cycle). Push 3,2,1,0 to stack. Stack: [3,2,1,0]. Step 2: Reverse graph: 1→0, 2→1, 3→2, 0→3. Step 3: Second DFS on reversed graph. Pop 0: DFS from 0 visits 0→3→2→1 (all vertices). One SCC containing all vertices: [[0,1,2,3]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Second DFS on Transposed Graph
ladders.desc: Perform DFS on the reversed graph using stack ordering to extract SCCs.
ladders.explanation: This is the second DFS pass in Kosaraju's algorithm, where we actually identify the SCCs. After obtaining the stack from the first DFS (ordered by finishing times), we reverse the graph and process vertices in stack order (LIFO). For each unvisited vertex popped from the stack, we perform DFS on the reversed graph. All vertices reached in this DFS form one SCC. The intuition: In the reversed graph, edges between different SCCs are reversed, so starting from a 'sink' SCC (one with no outgoing edges to other SCCs) in the original graph ensures we only visit vertices within that SCC. The stack ordering guarantees we process vertices in decreasing order of finishing times, which corresponds to processing sink SCCs first. Time complexity for this step is O(V+E) for DFS traversal. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and stack storage. Common mistakes include not resetting visited array, incorrect stack popping order, or misunderstanding why reversed graph is used. This step directly produces the final SCCs by grouping reachable vertices in the reversed graph.

examples.trace: Initialize visited all False. Pop 4: DFS from 4 visits 4 only → SCC {4}. Pop 3: DFS from 3 visits 3 only → SCC {3}. Pop 2: DFS from 2 visits 2→1→0 → SCC {0,1,2}. Result: [[0,1,2], [3], [4]].
Pop 0: DFS from 0 visits 0→3→2→1 (all vertices) → one SCC {0,1,2,3}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — First DFS for Finishing Time Order
ladders.desc: Perform DFS on original graph to get vertices in order of decreasing finishing times.
ladders.explanation: This is the first DFS pass in Kosaraju's algorithm. We perform a standard depth-first search on the original graph, pushing each vertex onto a stack when we finish processing it (i.e., after exploring all its descendants). This creates a LIFO order where vertices with later finishing times are at the top. The intuition: In a directed graph, vertices in 'sink' SCCs (those with no outgoing edges to other SCCs) will finish first in DFS. By reversing this order (using stack LIFO), we get vertices in decreasing order of finishing times, which means we process sink SCCs last. This ordering is crucial because when we later process the reversed graph, we want to start from sink SCCs first to avoid crossing between SCCs. Time complexity is O(V+E) for DFS. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and stack. Common mistakes include not pushing vertices after full exploration, using BFS instead of DFS, or incorrect stack ordering. This step prepares the order for the second DFS pass.

examples.trace: Start DFS at 0: visit 0→1→2 (back to 0, cycle). Finish 2 → push 2. Finish 1 → push 1. Finish 0 → push 0. Continue DFS from 3: visit 3→4. Finish 4 → push 4. Finish 3 → push 3. Stack order (bottom to top): 0,1,2,3,4.
Start DFS at 0: 0→1→2→3→0 (cycle). Finish 3 → push 3. Finish 2 → push 2. Finish 1 → push 1. Finish 0 → push 0. Stack: 0,1,2,3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Reversal (Transposition)
ladders.desc: Create a new graph with all edges reversed.
ladders.explanation: Graph reversal (transposition) is creating a new graph where every edge u→v becomes v→u. This operation preserves strongly connected components but changes reachability between different SCCs. In Kosaraju's algorithm, we reverse the graph between the two DFS passes. The intuition: In the original graph, edges between SCCs go from source SCCs to sink SCCs. After reversal, these edges go from sink SCCs to source SCCs. This means that when we perform DFS on the reversed graph starting from a sink SCC vertex, we cannot reach vertices in other SCCs (because those edges are now reversed). This isolation allows us to cleanly extract one SCC at a time. Time complexity is O(V+E) to iterate through all edges. Space complexity is O(V+E) for the new adjacency list. Common mistakes include not handling multiple edges correctly, modifying the original graph in-place, or incorrect adjacency list construction. This step is essential for the second DFS to work correctly.

examples.trace: Original edges: 0→1 becomes 1→0. 1→2 becomes 2→1. 2→0 becomes 0→2. 1→3 becomes 3→1. 3→4 becomes 4→3. Result: adjacency lists: 0: [2], 1: [0], 2: [1], 3: [1], 4: [3].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Depth-First Search (DFS) on Directed Graph
ladders.desc: Traverse a directed graph using DFS to visit all reachable vertices.
ladders.explanation: Depth-First Search (DFS) is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For directed graphs, DFS starts at a source vertex and recursively visits all unvisited neighbors. We maintain a visited array to avoid cycles and infinite loops. The key property for Kosaraju's algorithm is finishing times: when we finish exploring a vertex (after all its descendants are visited), we record this order. This is done by pushing the vertex onto a stack after the recursive calls return. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack and visited array. Common mistakes include not marking vertices as visited before recursion, infinite recursion in cycles, or incorrect adjacency list handling. DFS is the core traversal mechanism used in both passes of Kosaraju's algorithm.

examples.trace: Start at 0 (mark visited). From 0, go to 1 (mark visited). From 1, go to 2 (mark visited). From 2, go to 0 (already visited, backtrack). Finish 2, finish 1, finish 0. Visited: 0,1,2.
Start at 0. Go to 1 (mark). From 1, go to 3 (mark). Backtrack from 3, backtrack from 1. From 0, go to 2 (mark). Visited: 0,1,3,2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strongly Connected Components (SCCs)
ladders.desc: Understand what SCCs are in directed graphs.
ladders.explanation: A strongly connected component (SCC) in a directed graph is a maximal subgraph where every vertex is reachable from every other vertex. This means for any two vertices u and v in the SCC, there exists a path from u to v AND a path from v to u. SCCs partition the graph into equivalence classes based on mutual reachability. For example, in a cycle, all vertices in the cycle form one SCC. A single vertex with no edges is its own SCC. The intuition: SCCs represent clusters of tightly connected vertices. Finding SCCs is important for understanding graph connectivity, solving 2-SAT problems, and analyzing networks. In Kosaraju's algorithm, we use the property that reversing the graph preserves SCCs (edges within an SCC remain, but edges between SCCs reverse direction). This allows us to isolate SCCs during traversal. Common mistakes include confusing SCCs with weakly connected components or not understanding maximality. This foundational concept is why we need algorithms like Kosaraju's.

examples.trace: Check reachability: 0→1→2→0, so 0 reaches 1 and 2. 1→2→0→1, so 1 reaches 0 and 2. 2→0→1→2, so 2 reaches 0 and 1. All vertices mutually reachable → one SCC.
Vertices 0 and 1: 0→1 and 1→0 → mutually reachable → SCC {0,1}. Vertices 2 and 3: 2→3 and 3→2 → mutually reachable → SCC {2,3}. No paths between these groups → separate SCCs.

approaches.name: Tarjan's Algorithm O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use a single DFS with low-link values and a stack to track SCCs by identifying back edges and root nodes. It efficiently finds all SCCs in linear time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Strongly Connected Components Using Tarjan's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement Tarjan's algorithm to find all strongly connected components in a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: Tarjan's algorithm finds all strongly connected components (SCCs) in a directed graph using a single DFS traversal with low-link values and a stack. The intuition is that during DFS, we assign each node a discovery time (index) and track the lowest discovery time reachable from that node (low-link). When we find a node whose low-link equals its discovery time, we've found the root of an SCC and can pop nodes from the stack until we reach this root. This works because in an SCC, all nodes are mutually reachable, so they form a cycle where low-link values propagate to the root. The algorithm runs in O(V+E) time and O(V) space. It connects to the key subroutine by implementing the complete DFS with SCC tracking. Common mistakes include not resetting visited flags correctly or mishandling stack operations. Real code pattern: Initialize arrays for indices, low-links, and stack status; perform DFS; when low-link equals index, pop SCC from stack.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start DFS at node 0, index=0, low=0, push 0. Step 2: Visit 1, index=1, low=1, push 1. Step 3: Visit 2, index=2, low=2, push 2. Step 4: From 2, edge to 0 (already visited, in stack), update low[2]=min(2,0)=0. Step 5: Backtrack to 1, update low[1]=min(1,0)=0. Step 6: From 1, edge to 3, index=3, low=3, push 3. Step 7: 3 has no outgoing edges, low[3]=3 equals index, pop SCC [3]. Step 8: Backtrack to 1, then 0, low[0]=min(0,0)=0 equals index, pop SCC [2,1,0] (reverse stack-pop order inside SCC). Final SCCs: [[0,1,2], [3]].

Step 1: Start at node 0, index=0, low=0, push 0. Step 2: Visit 1, index=1, low=1, push 1. Step 3: Visit 2, index=2, low=2, push 2. Step 4: 2 has no edges, low=2 equals index, pop SCC [2]. Step 5: Backtrack to 1, low=1 equals index, pop SCC [1]. Step 6: Backtrack to 0, low=0 equals index, pop SCC [0]. Final SCCs: [[0], [1], [2]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Low-Link Updates
ladders.desc: Implement the DFS function that assigns indices and updates low-link values for nodes.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs a depth-first search on a graph, assigning each node a unique discovery index and maintaining a low-link value that represents the smallest index reachable from that node. The intuition is that by tracking low-links during DFS, we can identify cycles and root nodes of SCCs. Steps: Initialize index counter; for each unvisited node, call DFS helper; in DFS, set index and low-link to current index, push node to stack, mark as in-stack; recursively visit neighbors; if neighbor is unvisited, recurse and update low-link with neighbor's low-link; if neighbor is visited and in stack, update low-link with neighbor's index; after visiting all neighbors, if low-link equals index, pop nodes from stack until current node to form an SCC. Time: O(V+E) for DFS traversal; space: O(V) for arrays and stack. This feeds into L0 by providing the core DFS logic. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark nodes as in-stack or not updating low-links correctly. Real code pattern: def dfs(u): set index[u]=low[u]=curr_index, push u, for v in adj[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs(v); low[u]=min(low[u], low[v]); else if in_stack[v]: low[u]=min(low[u], index[v]).

examples.input: Graph: nodes 0,1,2 with edges 0->1, 1->2, 2->0, start DFS at 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Visit 0, index=0, low=0, push 0. Step 2: Visit 1, index=1, low=1, push 1. Step 3: Visit 2, index=2, low=2, push 2. Step 4: From 2, edge to 0 (visited, in stack), update low[2]=min(2,0)=0. Step 5: Backtrack to 1, update low[1]=min(1,0)=0. Step 6: Backtrack to 0, update low[0]=min(0,0)=0. Final: indices=[0,1,2], low=[0,0,0].

examples.input: Graph: nodes 0,1,2 with edges 0->1, 1->2, start DFS at 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Visit 0, index=0, low=0, push 0. Step 2: Visit 1, index=1, low=1, push 1. Step 3: Visit 2, index=2, low=2, push 2. Step 4: 2 has no edges, low stays 2. Step 5: Backtrack to 1, low stays 1. Step 6: Backtrack to 0, low stays 0. Final: indices=[0,1,2], low=[0,1,2].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identifying SCC Roots Using Low-Link Equality
ladders.desc: Determine when a node is the root of an SCC by comparing its low-link value to its discovery index.
ladders.explanation: The central insight of Tarjan's algorithm is that a node is the root of a strongly connected component if its low-link value equals its discovery index. Intuition: In an SCC, all nodes are mutually reachable, forming a cycle. During DFS, the low-link of each node propagates to the smallest index in that cycle. The root is the first node discovered in the SCC, so its index is the smallest in the cycle, and thus low-link equals index. For other nodes in the SCC, low-link will be less than index because they can reach the root. Steps: During DFS, after processing all neighbors, check if low[u] == index[u]; if true, u is an SCC root. Then, pop nodes from stack until u is popped; these nodes form the SCC. This logic is efficient because it uses a single DFS pass. Time: O(1) per node for the check; space: O(1). It connects to L1 by being the condition that triggers SCC formation. Common mistakes: Misinterpreting low-link updates or checking at the wrong time. Real code pattern: After DFS recursion: if low[u] == index[u]: scc = []; while True: v = stack.pop(); scc.append(v); if v == u: break; output scc.

examples.input: Node with index=2, low-link=2, stack has nodes [0,1,2]
examples.trace: Step 1: After DFS, check low[2]==index[2]: 2==2 is true. Step 2: Pop from stack: pop 2, add to SCC. Step 3: Since popped node equals current node (2==2), break. SCC formed: [2].

examples.input: Node with index=0, low-link=0, stack has nodes [0,1,2]
examples.trace: Step 1: Check low[0]==index[0]: 0==0 is true. Step 2: Pop from stack: pop 2, add to SCC. Step 3: Pop 1, add to SCC. Step 4: Pop 0, add to SCC. Step 5: Since popped node equals current node (0==0), break. SCC formed: [2,1,0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Low-Link Values in a Graph
ladders.desc: Calculate the low-link value for each node, representing the smallest discovery index reachable from that node.
ladders.explanation: The low-link value of a node is the smallest discovery index that can be reached from that node, including itself, via DFS traversal. Intuition: It helps identify cycles and SCCs by tracking how far back in the DFS tree a node can reach. If a node can reach an ancestor, its low-link will be lower than its index, indicating it's part of a cycle. Steps: Initialize low-link equal to index when node is first visited. During DFS, for each neighbor: if neighbor is unvisited, recurse and update low-link with min(current low, neighbor's low); if neighbor is visited and currently in the stack (indicating a back edge), update low-link with min(current low, neighbor's index). This ensures low-link propagates through cycles. Time: O(E) for edge processing; space: O(V) for storing low-links. This feeds into L2 by providing the values needed for root identification. Common mistakes: Updating with wrong value or not checking if neighbor is in stack. Real code pattern: low[u] = index[u]; for v in adj[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs(v); low[u] = min(low[u], low[v]); else if in_stack[v]: low[u] = min(low[u], index[v]).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, low[0]=0. Step 2: Visit 1, low[1]=1. Step 3: Visit 2, low[2]=2. Step 4: From 2, edge to 0 (visited, in stack), update low[2]=min(2,0)=0. Step 5: Backtrack to 1, update low[1]=min(1,0)=0. Step 6: Backtrack to 0, update low[0]=min(0,0)=0. Final: low=[0,0,0].
Step 1: Start at 0, low[0]=0. Step 2: Visit 1, low[1]=1. Step 3: Visit 2, low[2]=2. Step 4: No back edges, so low values stay. Final: low=[0,1,2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Performing Depth-First Search on a Directed Graph
ladders.desc: Execute a DFS traversal to visit all nodes in a directed graph, tracking discovery order.
ladders.explanation: Depth-first search is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. Intuition: It systematically visits all nodes by following edges deeply, which is efficient for exploring connectivity. Steps: Initialize a visited array; for each unvisited node, call DFS helper; in DFS, mark node as visited, process it (e.g., assign discovery index), then recursively visit all unvisited neighbors. For directed graphs, follow outgoing edges. Time: O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once; space: O(V) for recursion stack and visited array. This feeds into L3 by providing the traversal framework for computing low-links. Common mistakes: Not handling disconnected graphs or infinite recursion in cycles. Real code pattern: visited = [False]*n; def dfs(u): visited[u]=True; for v in adj[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs(v).

examples.input: Graph: nodes 0,1,2 with edges 0->1, 1->2, start DFS at 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited. Step 2: From 0, edge to 1, visit 1, mark visited. Step 3: From 1, edge to 2, visit 2, mark visited. Step 4: No more edges, backtrack to 1, then 0. Visited: 0,1,2.

examples.input: Graph: nodes 0,1,2 with edges 0->1, 2->0, start DFS at 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited. Step 2: From 0, edge to 1, visit 1, mark visited. Step 3: No more edges from 1, backtrack to 0. Node 2 is unvisited but not reachable from 0. Visited: 0,1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strongly Connected Components in Directed Graphs
ladders.desc: Define and identify strongly connected components as maximal sets of mutually reachable nodes.
ladders.explanation: A strongly connected component (SCC) in a directed graph is a maximal subgraph where every node is reachable from every other node. Intuition: It represents clusters of nodes that form cycles or are tightly connected, such that you can travel from any node to any other within the SCC. This is foundational because many graph problems involve analyzing connectivity. Steps to identify: For a set of nodes, check if for every pair (u,v), there is a path from u to v and from v to u. In practice, algorithms like Tarjan's automate this. Key properties: SCCs partition the graph; a single node can be an SCC if it has no cycles. Time: Naively O(V^3) for checking all pairs; space: O(1). This feeds into L4 by motivating why DFS is used for efficient SCC finding. Common mistakes: Confusing with weakly connected components or not considering maximality. Real code pattern: Concept only—no code, but think: scc = set of nodes where all pairs are mutually reachable.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check node 0: path to 1 (0->1), to 2 (0->1->2); from 1 to 0 (1->2->0); from 2 to 0 (2->0). Step 2: Similarly, all pairs are mutually reachable. Step 3: No larger set includes these nodes, so SCC is {0,1,2}.
Step 1: Check node 0: path to 1 exists, but from 1 to 0 does not. Step 2: So 0 and 1 are not in same SCC. Step 3: Each alone forms an SCC: [[0],[1]].
